metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Mathematics 8 extends students' number sense through perfect squares and cubes, proportional reasoning, and fractions, while building algebraic thinking through linear relations and two-step equations. Students deepen their understanding of geometry and measurement with the Pythagorean theorem and 3D objects, explore probability and data, and apply mathematical thinking to financial literacy. First Peoples mathematical knowledge, including cultural number systems and spatial reasoning, is integrated throughout.
metadata.general_description: An intermediate mathematics course developing number sense, proportional reasoning, algebra, geometry, data analysis, and financial literacy — building strong foundations for senior mathematics pathways.

big_ideas.elaboration: Understanding how numbers — including perfect squares and cubes, fractions, and percents — represent quantities and relationships is foundational to all mathematical reasoning.

big_ideas.statement: Computational fluency develops from a strong sense of number.
big_ideas.elaboration: Fluency with operations — especially with fractions and integers — develops through understanding, not just memorization, building efficiency, flexibility, and accuracy in computation.

big_ideas.statement: We use patterns to represent identified regularities and to make generalizations.
big_ideas.elaboration: Identifying patterns in number sequences and algebraic expressions allows us to generalize mathematical relationships and describe them with rules, tables, and graphs.

big_ideas.statement: We can describe, measure, and compare spatial relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Geometric thinking — including the Pythagorean theorem, surface area, volume, and 3D object visualization — allows us to describe, measure, and solve spatial problems.

big_ideas.statement: Analyzing data and chance enables us to compare and interpret.
big_ideas.elaboration: Collecting, organizing, and interpreting data — along with reasoning about probability — builds the capacity to make informed decisions based on evidence.

competencies.raw: Use logic and patterns to solve puzzles and play games; apply reasoning and logic to explore, analyze, and apply mathematical ideas; estimate reasonably; demonstrate and apply mental math strategies; use tools or technology to explore patterns and test conjectures; model mathematics in contextualized experiences.
i_can_statements: I can use logical thinking and pattern recognition to solve mathematical puzzles.
I can estimate answers to problems and judge whether my results are reasonable.
I can use mental math strategies flexibly to calculate efficiently.
I can use tools and technology to explore patterns and test my conjectures.
I can model real-world situations with mathematical representations.

competencies.raw: Apply multiple strategies to solve problems in abstract and contextualized situations; develop and demonstrate mathematical understanding through play, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize to explore mathematical concepts; engage in problem-solving connected to place, story, and cultural practices.
i_can_statements: I can choose and apply multiple strategies to solve problems — including drawing diagrams, working backwards, and guess-and-check.
I can visualize and sketch diagrams to help me understand mathematical problems.
I can connect mathematical problem-solving to real-world situations, stories, and cultural contexts.

competencies.raw: Use mathematical vocabulary and language in discussions; explain and justify mathematical ideas and decisions; communicate mathematical thinking in multiple ways; represent mathematical ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms.
i_can_statements: I can use correct mathematical vocabulary when discussing problems.
I can explain my thinking and justify my solutions in multiple ways.
I can represent mathematical ideas using objects, diagrams, tables, graphs, and symbols.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect mathematical concepts to other areas and personal interests; use mathematical arguments to support personal choices; incorporate First Peoples worldviews and perspectives.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my mathematical thinking and identify what I understand and what I need to work on.
I can connect math concepts to other subject areas and to things I care about.
I can incorporate First Peoples mathematical knowledge, including cultural contexts and spatial reasoning, into my learning.

areas.name: Number
topics: Percents: greater than 100%, fractional percents, and decimal percents
Proportional reasoning: rate, ratio, percent, and fraction problems
Operations with fractions: addition, subtraction, multiplication, division (including mixed numbers)

areas.name: Patterning and Algebra
areas.raw: We use patterns to represent identified regularities and to make generalizations.
topics: Discrete linear relations: tables of values, equations, and graphs
Expressions and substitution: evaluating expressions with substitution
Two-step linear equations with integers: solving and verifying solutions

areas.name: Geometry and Measurement
areas.raw: We can describe, measure, and compare spatial relationships.
topics: Surface area of regular solids: right prisms and right cylinders
Volume of regular solids: right prisms and right cylinders
Pythagorean theorem: finding missing sides in right triangles
3D objects: constructing, drawing views (top, front, side), and nets

areas.name: Data and Probability
areas.raw: Analyzing data and chance enables us to compare and interpret.
topics: Central tendency: mean, median, mode — calculation and interpretation
Theoretical probability with two independent events: sample space, tree diagrams, and tables

areas.name: Financial Literacy
areas.raw: Financial literacy: best buys using unit pricing, proportions, and coupons.
topics: Unit pricing: comparing value for money
Using coupons and discounts: percent off and flat rate savings
